title: National Minimum Wage — Workplace Relations Commission
content:
The National Minimum Wage is the lowest hourly rate of pay that employers can legally pay employees in Ireland. It is set by the Government and reviewed annually.

Current rates (2025)
- National Minimum Wage: €13.50 per hour (effective from 1 January 2025)
- This applies to all employees aged 20 and over

Young workers and sub-minimum rates
Certain categories of workers may be paid a reduced rate:
- Aged 18 or 19: 90% of the National Minimum Wage = €12.15 per hour
- Under 18: 70% of the National Minimum Wage = €9.45 per hour

These reduced rates were scheduled to be phased out by 2026 — check the most recent Government announcement for any updates.

Who is entitled to the minimum wage
Most employees are entitled to the National Minimum Wage, including:
- Full-time, part-time, temporary, and casual workers
- Employees on fixed-term contracts
- Agency workers (paid at least minimum wage by the agency)

Who is NOT covered
- The self-employed
- Close family members of employers (spouse, civil partner, children)
- Registered apprentices in a statutory apprenticeship scheme (different rates apply)

Employment Regulation Orders
Certain sectors have their own minimum wage rates set above the National Minimum Wage under Employment Regulation Orders (EROs). Sectors with their own rates include Security, Contract Cleaning, Hairdressing, Retail Grocery, and Catering. Check if your sector has an ERO that applies higher minimum rates.

Working out if you are being paid correctly
To check whether you are receiving at least the minimum wage, calculate your average hourly rate:
1. Add up all your pay in a pay period (before tax but after pension contributions)
2. Divide by the total hours worked
3. This must be at least €13.50 per hour

Paid below minimum wage
If you believe you are being paid below the minimum wage:
1. Raise it with your employer first
2. If not resolved, refer a complaint to the WRC. You can do this online at workplacerelations.ie
3. A WRC Inspector may investigate and your employer may be required to pay arrears

Statutory entitlements are separate
Receiving the minimum wage does not affect your other statutory entitlements — annual leave, public holidays, notice periods, and statutory redundancy are calculated separately.

title: Annual Leave and Public Holidays — Workplace Relations Commission
content:
All employees in Ireland are entitled to paid annual leave under the Organisation of Working Time Act 1997. The minimum entitlement is 4 working weeks per year.

Calculating annual leave
Your annual leave entitlement is calculated using whichever of these methods gives the greatest leave:
1. 4 working weeks per leave year (if you worked at least 1,365 hours in the leave year)
2. 1/3 of a working week for each calendar month in which you worked at least 117 hours
3. 8% of hours worked in a leave year, subject to a maximum of 4 working weeks

For part-time workers, Method 3 (8% of hours worked) is most common.

When leave is taken
Employers decide when annual leave is taken, but must take account of employees' needs. Leave dates should be confirmed at least one month in advance. If an employer refuses leave in a particular period, they cannot refuse all leave — you must be given the opportunity to take 2 continuous weeks during the summer months if you request it.

Pay during annual leave
You must be paid your normal weekly wages during annual leave. This includes regular overtime, regular shift allowances, and other regular payments. Pay must be given before the start of the leave period, unless other arrangements are agreed.

Public holidays
There are 10 public holidays in Ireland:
- New Year's Day (1 January)
- St Brigid's Day (first Monday in February)
- St Patrick's Day (17 March)
- Easter Monday
- First Monday in May
- First Monday in June
- First Monday in August
- Last Monday in October
- Christmas Day (25 December)
- St Stephen's Day (26 December)

Entitlement on public holidays
For each public holiday, employees are entitled to one of:
- A paid day off on the public holiday itself
- An additional day of annual leave
- An additional day's pay
- A paid day off within a month of the public holiday

Part-time workers
Part-time workers are entitled to public holiday benefits proportional to the hours they work. If a public holiday falls on a day you do not normally work, you are still entitled to 1/5 of your normal weekly wage as compensation.

Annual leave on termination
If your employment ends and you have unused annual leave, your employer must pay you in lieu of the leave you did not take.

title: Statutory Redundancy Payments — Workplace Relations Commission
content:
Statutory redundancy is a payment that employees are legally entitled to when they lose their job due to redundancy. Redundancy occurs when an employer reduces their workforce — for business reasons, not because of the individual employee's conduct or performance.

Eligibility
You are entitled to statutory redundancy if:
- You have been continuously employed for at least 2 years
- You are being made redundant (the job, not just you personally, is being eliminated)
- You are 16 years of age or over
- You work under a contract of employment (full-time, part-time, or fixed-term)

How redundancy pay is calculated
Statutory redundancy is calculated as:
- 2 weeks' gross pay for each full year of service
- Plus 1 bonus week (regardless of length of service)
- Pay is capped at €600 per week for the purposes of this calculation

Example: 5 years of service, earning €500 per week.
Redundancy = (5 years × 2 weeks × €500) + (1 bonus week × €500) = €5,000 + €500 = €5,500

Example: 5 years of service, earning €900 per week (above cap).
Redundancy is calculated on €600 (the cap), not €900.
Redundancy = (5 × 2 × €600) + (1 × €600) = €6,000 + €600 = €6,600

Notice period
In addition to redundancy pay, you are entitled to a minimum notice period:
- Less than 2 years: 1 week
- 2-5 years: 2 weeks
- 5-10 years: 4 weeks
- 10-15 years: 6 weeks
- Over 15 years: 8 weeks

Alternative to working notice
Your employer can pay you in lieu of notice — you leave immediately but receive pay for the notice period.

Redundancy process
Your employer must give you formal written notice of redundancy using form RP50. This must be given at least 2 weeks before the effective date of redundancy. You then have 52 weeks from the date of redundancy to claim your payment if it is not made.

Disputing redundancy payment
If your employer fails to pay statutory redundancy:
1. Refer a complaint to the WRC online at workplacerelations.ie
2. A WRC Adjudicator will hear the case
3. If your employer is insolvent, you can apply to the Department of Enterprise for payment from the Social Insurance Fund

title: Unfair Dismissal — Rights and How to Claim — Workplace Relations Commission
content:
Unfair dismissal is where an employee is dismissed from their job without a fair reason or without following fair procedures. The Unfair Dismissals Acts 1977-2015 protect most employees in Ireland.

Who is covered
Most employees are covered by unfair dismissal legislation once they have completed 1 year of continuous service with the same employer. You are covered if you:
- Have at least 1 year of continuous service
- Work under a contract of employment (full-time, part-time, fixed-term)
- Are working in Ireland

Exceptions (1-year minimum does not apply)
The 1-year service requirement does not apply if you were dismissed for:
- A reason connected to pregnancy or maternity
- A reason connected to trade union membership or activities
- Exercising a right under the National Minimum Wage Act

What counts as unfair dismissal
A dismissal is unfair if:
- Your employer cannot show a valid reason for the dismissal
- Your employer did not follow fair procedures before dismissing you
- The reason was discriminatory (related to pregnancy, trade union membership, etc.)

Fair reasons for dismissal
An employer can fairly dismiss an employee for:
- Capability (the employee cannot do the job)
- Competence (persistent poor performance, after fair warning)
- Conduct (misconduct — must follow disciplinary procedures)
- Redundancy (genuine reduction in workforce)
- Legal requirements (the employee cannot legally continue in the role)

Fair procedures
Even where there is a valid reason, the employer must follow fair procedures:
1. Give the employee full details of the complaint or performance issue
2. Allow the employee to respond and put their case
3. Allow the employee to be accompanied by a colleague or trade union representative
4. Consider the response before making a decision
5. Allow an appeal of the decision

How to make a claim
If you were unfairly dismissed, you must refer your case to the WRC within 6 months of the date of dismissal. This can be extended to 12 months if there were exceptional circumstances preventing you from claiming within 6 months.

Apply online at workplacerelations.ie.

Remedies for unfair dismissal
A WRC Adjudicator can order one of three remedies:
1. Reinstatement (your job back, with back pay as if you were never dismissed)
2. Re-engagement (return to employment in the same or similar role, without necessarily backdated pay)
3. Compensation (up to 2 years' gross pay)

The most common remedy is compensation. The amount awarded depends on the loss suffered by the employee and whether they took steps to find new employment.

title: Working Hours and Rest Periods — Workplace Relations Commission
content:
Under the Organisation of Working Time Act 1997, employees in Ireland have the right to limits on maximum working hours and to minimum rest periods between and during work shifts.

Maximum working hours
You cannot be required to work more than an average of 48 hours per week. This average is calculated over a reference period of 4 months (or 6 months for certain sectors). Some industries with special provisions (such as agriculture, transport, and emergency services) have different arrangements.

The 48-hour average means you can sometimes work more than 48 hours in a particular week, as long as the average over 4 months does not exceed 48 hours.

Daily rest
You are entitled to a minimum of 11 consecutive hours off in every 24-hour period. For example, if you finish work at 10pm, your employer should not ask you to start again before 9am the next day.

Weekly rest
You are entitled to at least 24 consecutive hours off per week (on top of the daily rest entitlement). This is usually provided on Sunday, but the specific day can vary by agreement.

Breaks during work
- You are entitled to a 15-minute break if you work 4.5 hours or more without a break
- You are entitled to a 30-minute break if you work 6 hours or more without a break
- The 30-minute break can include the 15-minute break
- Your employer can specify when the break is taken, but it must fall within the working time (not at the start or end of a shift)

Night workers
If you work at night (during a period that includes midnight to 7am), you have additional protections:
- Your average working time must not exceed 8 hours in a 24-hour period
- Night workers doing hazardous work are subject to a strict 8-hour daily limit (not an average)

Sunday premium
If you work on Sundays and your pay does not already account for Sunday working, you are entitled to a Sunday premium. This does not have to be a set rate — it can be agreed between you and your employer, or follow a collective agreement. If there is no agreement, you are entitled to reasonable compensation for Sunday work.

Recording hours
Employers must keep records of employees' working hours for at least 3 years. These records must be available for inspection by a WRC Inspector.

Complaining about working time breaches
If you believe your working hours rights are being violated:
1. Raise it with your employer or HR
2. If not resolved, refer a complaint to the WRC at workplacerelations.ie
3. There is a 6-month time limit to make a complaint (extendable to 12 months in exceptional circumstances)
